data.title: Almost 7 in 10 say Epstein files show powerful people rarely held accountable: Poll
data.description:
Most Americans say they believe the files connected to convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein show that wealthy, powerful people are rarely held accountable, according to a new Reuters/Ipsos poll released on Wednesday.

The poll found that nearly 7 in 10 respondents, or 69 percent of Americans, believe their views were captured “very well” or “extremely well” by a statement saying the Epstein files “show that powerful people in the U.S. are rarely held accountable for their actions.”

Pollsters also found that 17 percent of respondents believe the statement aligns with their views “somewhat well.” Eleven percent of respondents said it did not reflect their views.

Most Democrats and Republicans, at 89 and 86 percent respectively, strongly or somewhat agree that the rich and powerful are not held to account.

Reuters/Ipsos also found that 53 percent of respondents “strongly agree” that the files “have lowered my trust in political and business leaders,” along with 24 percent who “somewhat agree.” Among Republicans, 33 percent “strongly agree” and 35 percent “somewhat agree.” The poll found that 71 percent of Democrats “strongly agree,” while 17 percent “somewhat agree.”

The Department of Justice (DOJ) released its final tranche of files — 3 million emails, photos, documents and other materials — last month. The files also carried a plethora of redactions, which were intended by law to hide the identities of victims, survivors and anyone under criminal investigation. Members of Congress have pressured the DOJ to reveal several redacted names in the files after lawmakers found that there were several heavy “unnecessary” redactions in the documents.

“Release the full files,” Rep. Ro Khanna (D-Calif.) one of the co-sponsors of the Epstein Files Transparency Act, wrote on the social platform X on Saturday. “Stop protecting predators. Redact only the survivors’ names.”

Critics argue that the Trump administration has not been transparent in its handling of the Epstein files and its investigation into Epstein’s crimes.

DOJ officials have fought back, with Deputy Attorney General Todd Blanche on Friday accusing lawmakers of forcing the department to unmask “completely random people” with no connection to Epstein and his crimes.

The Reuters/Ipsos survey was conducted Feb. 13-16 and included 1,117 respondents. The margin of error is 3 percentage points.

data.title: Greene on Trump 'traitor' label: 'I'm not in his cult'
data.description:
Former Rep. Marjorie Taylor Greene (R-Ga.) on Tuesday responded to President Trump labeling her a “traitor,” repeating her accusation that Trump is trying to hide information related to late sex offender and financier Jeffrey Epstein.

“Him calling me that has become a badge of honor because I’m not in his cult, I refused to bow to his demands to cover up the Epstein files to protect his friends, and I stopped fighting the hardest for a team that intentionally refuses to win,” Greene wrote in a post on X.

The president earlier this month endorsed Clay Fuller to replace Greene, who resigned from her House seat representing Georgia’s 14th congressional district at the start of the year.

Speaking to reporters on Air Force One on Monday, Trump said, “Well, we have a lot of people that want to take Marjorie ‘Traitor’ Greene’s place.”

The former Georgia representative, once a loyal Trump supporter, has become increasingly vocal in her criticism of the president, on issues ranging from the Epstein files to Trump’s military operations in foreign countries and Republicans failing to extend Affordable Care Act subsidies. 

Trump is expected to visit her congressional district on Thursday ahead of the March 10 special election to replace her.

Currently, 18 candidates are running to replace Greene. If no candidate receives more than 50 percent of the vote, a runoff election will be held April 7.

Fuller, a district attorney, came in fourth in the 2020 Republican primary that Greene won on her way to a first term in office.

“Clay Fuller has my Complete and Total Endorsement to be the next Representative from Georgia’s 14th Congressional District – HE WILL NOT LET YOU DOWN!” Trump wrote in a February Truth Social post.

Fuller accepted Trump’s endorsement in a post on X and said it was “the honor of a lifetime.”

Greene warned earlier this month that the MAGA brand is all a “lie,” describing Trump’s first year back in office as focused on obliging wealthy supporters.

“I think people are realizing it was all a lie. It was a big lie for the people. What MAGA is really serving in this administration, who they’re serving, is their big donors,” Greene said in an interview with radio personality Kim Iversen.

data.title: Watch live: Jeffries gives remarks amid DHS shutdown
data.description:
House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries (D-N.Y.) will speak with reporters Wednesday afternoon as the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) shutdown stretches into its fifth day.

Democrats and the White House have been engaged in a back-and-forth over lawmakers’ push to reform DHS’s tactics when carrying out President Trump’s mass deportation agenda. Dems in the Senate offered up a new proposal on Tuesday, though few details were given.

The pressure comes after two fatal shootings in Minnesota by federal agents earlier this year raised concerns over Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem’s handling of Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) operations and its use of force. The criticism has led to calls for Noem to resign or be held accountable.

With the State of the Union address coming up next week, many Democrats are also eyeing ways to broadcast their defiance of Trump’s policies.

Jeffries’ comments also come a day after it was revealed that DHS spokesperson Tricia McLaughlin is stepping down from her role amid the turmoil.

The press conference is scheduled to begin at 12:30 p.m. EST.

Watch the live video above.

data.title: DC Water provides updates 1 month after massive sewage spill into Potomac
data.description:
WASHINGTON (DC News Now) — On Tuesday, the D.C. council received its first briefing with DC Water after the massive sewage overflow into the Potomac River.

The Potomac Interceptor initially collapsed on Jan. 19. The sewage line, which is 72 inches in diameter, carries 60 million gallons of wastewater daily from Maryland, Virginia and D.C.

Following the collapse, several additional overflows occurred. In all, DC Water estimated 243 million gallons of sewage spilled into the Potomac River.

“I think there’s been a lack of clarity for the public to understand,” said councilmember Charles Allen, who organized the briefing. “We’ve got messages on the DOEE website that say don’t go in the water, don’t be exposed to the water, but no signage there. I was in Georgetown Park this last Saturday. There were kids splashing in the water, people fishing.”

“Our crews, contractors working under very, very challenging conditions over the past month, are nearing completion of an enhanced bypass system that will allow us to begin emergency repairs on the damaged section of pipe,” said DC Water CEO David Gadis.

Currently, a bypass system is in place that directs the sewage around the broken pipe, into the C&O Canal and then back into the pipe.

Gadis said emergency repairs should be completed in four to six weeks, though full repairs to the Potomac Interceptor will take six to nine months.

“I am also happy to report that we have not had any overflows into the Potomac River since February the 8th,” he said. “And, with additional pumps on site, we are cautiously optimistic that we will be able to contain the flow until repairs can be completed.”

Issues with the Potomac Interceptor at Lock 10 — where the collapse occurred — were not a total surprise. The portion of the pipe was slated for repairs this summer, with DC Water rating it a “3.5” in terms of severity.

“The scale is one to five, five being an emergency, four meaning it’s creeping into that area, three meaning it’s fair, so 3.5, it was fair. But we knew it was something we needed to put in our Capital Improvement Plan program. So, we put it in our CIP program for this summer. It happened to break on us before we got to it,” said Gadis. “We had slip lined a portion of the pipe before this and this was just the next segment of it, so we’ve been out there doing work on this Potomac Interceptor for some time.”

Slip lining is the installation of sections of new pipe inside the existing pipe.

There are other spots along the pipe that also need repair. That work will continue throughout the year. DC Water is also inspecting 20 areas along the pipe to ensure they’re in good condition.

At this point, the exact cause of the break has not been determined.

“Once we’re able to go down and remove part of the pipe or take a look at it, we’ll have a better view on what the cause of the break was. Right now, we would only be speculating,” said Gadis.

The cost of the emergency repair is also unclear.

Matt Brown, chief pperating officer for DC Water, could not confirm if customers will have their rates increased to cover the cost.

“We’re a regional wastewater authority. We allocate costs based on the volume that’s treated, so there are cost shares for all of our assets. At Blue Plains [Wastewater Treatment Plant], approximately 55 percent of the flow comes from outside of the District of Columbia. So then 55 percent of the costs are then borne by the users of the system who sent flow to D.C. We expect a similar cost share for this asset,” said Brown. “The jurisdictions outside of the D.C. are wholesale customers, so that’s something that they would need to speak to.”

Specific remediation plans are also unclear.

“We’ve committed to sharing our environmental remediation plans; we will do the same with costs. We are not at that phase yet. And when we do, we will share it,” said Kristen Williams, chief communications officer.

At this point, the federal government is not involved in the response, though Gadis said the EPA has been briefed.

A community meeting is currently being planned for next week.

D.C.’s Department of Energy and the Environment is advising people and pets to avoid contact with water from the Potomac River.

data.title: Trump is privatizing US imperialism: His Board of Peace is the next step
data.description:
It is only mid-February, and President Trump is already creating havoc across nearly every continent this year. Is this the madman theory in action, a return to crude imperialism or simply another turn in the long history of American overreach?

Each of these framings actually misses what is most dangerous about the moment. Trump’s foreign policy is not merely a revival of past abuses, but a structural shift in how power itself is exercised.

For decades, U.S. foreign policy, however misguided or destructive, was carried out through the state and justified in the name of national interest and ostensibly governed by law. Although that framework has been eroding for years, Trump’s innovation is to exploit its decay to replace public authority with private gain.

There is an insidious shift underway: The monetization and privatization of foreign policy for personal enrichment. In Venezuela, oil revenues are routed through opaque financial arrangements that bypassed Congress altogether, with funds placed in a Qatari bank. In Greenland, military and mineral narratives obscure a deeper attraction: a jurisdictional gray zone valued not only for resources, but as a lightly regulated space attractive to oligarchic networks seeking insulation from oversight.

The most explicit institutional expression of this pattern can be found in Trump’s “Board of Peace.” Initially presented as a mechanism to oversee a Gaza-Israel peace process, it has been widely chided as just another unserious Trump vanity project. Its formation lacked transparency and credibility, and appointments to it appeared arbitrary. 

Yet in truth, the board is not designed to succeed as a peace mechanism, but to function as an alternative authority structure. Its basic architecture concentrates power in a single individual, detaches decision-making from democratic or multilateral constraints and institutionalizes a model meant to outlive elections, with a mandate that goes even beyond Gaza and the Middle East. Trump’s chairmanship of this United Nations knockoff is not even tied to the presidency — it is apparently a lifetime appointment.

Trump’s objective is not effectiveness but displacement. The aim is not to reform multilateralism, but to make it obsolete by hollowing out the idea that peace and security are governed by states, law and collective responsibility. Despite claims that Trump is unconventional but “gets things done,” the realities of the Middle East show why the Board of Peace will not produce stability.

Like the Abraham Accords, this approach prioritizes transactional normalization while marginalizing Palestinian rights. It suppresses conflict temporarily by entrenching authoritarian bargains rather than addressing root causes. Ceasefire without accountability won’t bring durable peace. It only defers violence and leads to protracted conflict.

Including autocrats from Belarus to Egypt and beyond, the Board of Peace fits seamlessly into a regional environment where regime preservation is the overriding priority. Alliances and rifts are shaped less by ideology than by what sustains authoritarian rule, enforced through transactional loyalty rather than law. 

Trump is of course far from the first president to align himself with autocrats. What distinguishes his approach is the personalization of benefit.

Jared Kushner’s regional business interests, Middle East Envoy Steve Witkoff’s framing of the Middle East as a real-estate opportunity, offshore financial maneuvers beyond congressional oversight and resource ambitions from Venezuela to Greenland all reflect the same operating pattern.

A New York Times editorial board analysis estimates that President Trump and his family accrued at least $1.4 billion in personal profit within roughly one year of his second term, drawing from expanded crypto ventures, real-estate licensing, settlements and other business income that coincided with regulatory and diplomatic developments during his presidency.

This is not chaos — it is consolidation. Trump is moving rapidly to entrench power before institutional constraints tighten. His fears may not be unfounded: He warned that if Republicans lose their majority in Congress, impeachment could follow. Congress should not wait for elections, however, to act to restrain Trump’s attempt to unilaterally control and reshape foreign policy. 

The failure of recent War Powers Resolution votes, including a nearly-successful effort to advance a Senate measure that would block military action in Venezuela, could be disappointing but should not deter Congress from continuing the fight for their constitutional authority and oversight.

New initiatives are continuing to challenge Trump’s illegal wars. This includes the joint resolution by Sens. Tim Kaine (D-Va.) and Rand Paul (R-Ky.), worthy of lawmakers’ support, to direct the removal of U.S. Armed Forces from hostilities within or against Iran.

The value of lawmakers pushing such measures does not depend on passing a legally binding, veto-proof resolution constraining Trump, but on whether they can shape the political environment in which he operates. This is crucial, particularly as public oversight is increasingly obscured by suffocating free press through Trump’s intimidation or media capture by his billionaire supporters.

The question for Congress is whether it supports U.S. public resources, military force, diplomatic leverage and institutional legitimacy being deployed to serve personal power consolidation and private empire-building.

Attempting to restore a system that was neither equitable nor durable will not meet this moment. The goal is not to revive a broken order through nostalgia for the flawed status quo, but to prevent its replacement by something far worse.

Nancy Okail is president and CEO of the Center for International Policy.

data.title: Zelensky says Russia stalling in peace talks
data.description:
Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky on Wednesday accused Russia of trying to drag out negotiations to end the war, after trilateral talks that included the U.S. abruptly ended after two hours. 

Zelensky said the meetings that took place over two days in Geneva were “indeed difficult” and said he gave directions to his team “to do everything” so that talks are productive. 

“Yesterday’s meetings were indeed difficult, and we can state that Russia is trying to drag out negotiations that could already have reached the final stage,” Zelensky said.

“I thank the American side for its attention to detail and patience in conversations with the current representatives of Russia.”

President Trump has in recent days put pressure on Ukraine to “come to the table fast.” Russia’s war against Ukraine will hit its four-year mark next week. Trump has made it a priority to settle the war, and routinely put the blame on Zelensky as an obstacle to peace despite Russia’s invasion of Ukraine and its rejection of ceasefire offers.

Zelensky, in an interview with Axios on Tuesday evening, said he hopes Trump’s public remarks are “just his tactics and not the decision”.

Zelensky also criticized Moscow’s recently appointed negotiation lead, Vladimir Medinsky, as focusing on the “historical roots” of the war, that could further delay talks. 

“We don’t have time for all this shit. So we have to decide, and have to finish the war,” Zelensky told Axios. 

The Geneva talks marked the second round of face-to-face negotiations between the Russian, Ukrainian and American teams. The negotiators were divided into political and military tracks, where security parameters and mechanisms for implementing a halt to the fighting were discussed. 

Medinsky described the talks as “difficult, but businesslike,” and said another round will take place “in the near future.”

Rustem Umerov, Ukraine’s lead negotiator, described the discussions as “intensive and substantive.” 

“A number of issues were clarified, while others remained under additional coordination,” Umerov said. 

Trump’s Special Envoy Steve Witkoff posted on X that “both parties agreed to update their respective leaders and continue working towards a deal.”

The Ukrainian and American delegations also met separately with European officials, including representatives from the United Kingdom, France, Germany and Switzerland. 

Ukraine’s main priority is to receive security guarantees from the U.S. and Europe to guard against Russia violating any peace deal and launching another aggression. 

“We are working together with the team to bring real peace closer. The priority is security guarantees for Ukraine,” Zelensky said in a post on X. “Ukrainian representatives have clear directives on every aspect of the negotiations. I expect a detailed report following all the meetings.”

data.title: Trump immigration approval drops to new low: Survey
data.description:
Support for President Trump’s handling of immigration has fallen to its lowest level since he returned to the White House last year, according to a new survey.

The Reuters/Ipsos poll, released this week, found that 38 percent of U.S. adults approve of Trump’s immigration enforcement agenda, a 2-point drop from about a month ago. His approval rating on immigration peaked right at 50 percent in March of last year.

Another 55 percent of respondents said they disapprove of his policies, the survey shows.

The results mark the latest sign that the campaign issue that helped to deliver him back to the Oval Office could become a liability as the 2026 midterms approach.

Voters’ frustration is largely reflected in the polling of men, according to Reuters, which indicated that support among that demographic dipped in recent weeks. The latest survey showed Trump’s approval rating on immigration at 41 percent among men, compared to it hovering around 50 percent throughout last year.

Men, especially young men, represented a key voter group for Trump in the 2024 presidential election. Pew Research data indicated that the president made significant gains with the voting bloc under 50, a group that backed former President Biden by 10 points in 2020.

The tactics being used by the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) to execute Trump’s sweeping deportation agenda have also come under intense scrutiny, following the fatal shootings of two U.S. citizens during immigration enforcement operations in Minneapolis last month. Concerns have also mounted about the number of U.S. citizens and children reportedly being swept up in DHS raids.

Nearly two-thirds of Americans said in an Ipsos poll conducted in late January that Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) had gone “too far” in its efforts to deal with illegal immigration.

White House border czar Tom Homan announced last week that Operation Metro Surge had concluded in Minnesota but insisted that agents could “come back” if the situation changes.

That same Ipsos poll also found widespread agreement on the importance of conduct and training standards for federal immigration officers, a central issue in Democrats’ demands for reform within DHS.

Republicans and Democrats appear no closer to reaching an agreement that would fund DHS and end the partial government shutdown, which stretched into its fifth day on Wednesday. Senate Democrats, however, issued another counteroffer to the White House on Tuesday.

The Reuters/Ipsos poll was conducted Feb. 13-16 among 1,117 U.S. adults. It has a margin of error of 3 percentage points.

data.title: Google trying to recover footage from Nancy Guthrie's home cameras: Sheriff
data.description:
(NewsNation) — Investigators don’t yet have footage from various cameras at the home of missing 84-year-old Nancy Guthrie, but Google is trying to retrieve it, said Arizona’s Pima County Sheriff Chris Nanos.

Nanos told NewsNation’s Brian Entin on Tuesday that authorities so far have only obtained Google Nest video from the front of the house — footage that was able to be recovered even though Guthrie didn’t have an active subscription.

Investigators have asked Google whether additional video can be accessed, and, according to Nanos, the company said: “We don’t think we can get anything, but we’ll try.”

Nanos said he’s still hopeful more footage can be recovered, noting that the video already released generated nearly 5,000 tips. Google has described the retrieval process as delicate, he added.

“It’s like peeling paint —you have images over images over images,” Nanos said. “And you’ve got to peel back very easy because you might destroy the layer you wanted.”

With the search for Guthrie now in its third week, investigators are still looking for a breakthrough. DNA recovered from gloves found near her home did not produce a match in CODIS, the FBI’s national DNA database.

An FBI official told NewsNation on Tuesday that the DNA sample will now undergo genealogical testing.

Sheriff has ‘100% faith’ Nancy Guthrie case will be solved

Investigators are still working to determine what led to Guthrie’s disappearance — whether it was a burglary gone wrong or something more targeted involving her high-profile daughter, “Today” show co-host Savannah Guthrie.

“We recognize that anything is possible,” Nanos said. “The time to ask that question — motive — is when we actually know who’s done this.”

Authorities said Monday that Guthrie’s family had been cleared as potential suspects.

Guthrie was last seen at her Tucson home Jan. 31, but Nanos said he has “100% faith” that investigators will solve the case.

“If you were the guy, if you were that monster, you should be worried,” he said.

Authorities believe Guthrie was kidnapped in the late night or early morning hours before she was reported missing Feb. 1.

The FBI is offering a reward of up to $100,000 for information leading to Guthrie’s recovery or the arrest and conviction of anyone involved in her disappearance.

Anyone with “actionable information” is asked to contact 800-CALL-FBI or the Pima County Sheriff’s Department non-emergency line at 520-351-4900.

NewsNation’s Steph Whiteside contributed to this report.

data.title: Melania Trump's inaugural gown heading to the Smithsonian
data.description:
Melania Trump’s inaugural gown is getting a spot in the Smithsonian.

The Hervé Pierre-designed white gown that featured a dramatic black stripe that Trump wore at last year’s presidential inauguration will be showcased at the Smithsonian National Museum of American History’s First Ladies Gallery, it was announced Wednesday.

The creative process and fittings for the gown were also featured in Trump’s recent documentary film, “Melania.”

Melania Trump is expected to deliver remarks at a ceremony at the Washington museum on Friday.

It’s not the first time that one of Trump’s fashions has been featured in the Smithsonian. In 2017, the former model’s 2016 inaugural gown, also designed by Pierre, found a home in the museum’s collection of 1,000 first lady-related artifacts.

Having two inaugural gowns at the Smithsonian is “a rare distinction held by only a few first ladies in history,” according to the White House.

At the 2017 ceremony at the Smithsonian, Trump said, “I must tell you that it can be a daunting task to choose an outfit that will be [memorialized] and become a part of our nation’s story and forever history.”

data.title: Bolton: Trump should have set departure date for Iranian leader 'a long time ago'
data.description:
Former national security adviser John Bolton on Tuesday said President Trump should have set a departure date for Iran’s supreme leader a “long time ago” as indirect talks between the Washington and Tehran are set to continue.

“I think he should have done that a long time ago and indeed I think the U.S. has watched Iran sustain its regime, oppress its people, engage in supporting the world’s worst terrorists, and actively pursue a nuclear weapons program for a long, long time, and we’ve let it happen, and Israel and others have paid the consequences for it,” Bolton said during an appearance on NewsNation’s “On Balance.”

His remarks came hours after talks concluded in Geneva early Tuesday, with heavy pressure from the U.S. on Iran to dismantle its nuclear weapons program. Ahead of the conversation, Tehran also conducted exercises by firing live missiles toward the Strait of Hormuz.

“I wouldn’t dignify or legitimize negotiations with the Ayatollahs because you can’t trust them, they’ve never shown any inclination to give up their nuclear weapons program, and by allowing them to go through the fiction of negotiations, or even the 2015 nuclear agreement, we’ve simply made conditions in the Middle East and around the world much worse,” Bolton told host Leland Vittert. 

Last year, the U.S. struck three Iranian nuclear facilities, and the president has warned leaders the Middle East nation that he could take similar measures in the future if a deal is not reached to dismantle its nuclear program.

Trump told reporters last week that he thinks talks will be “successful” but warned that if they’re not, “it’s going to be a bad day for Iran, very bad.”

The president earlier this year issued a warning to Ayatollah Ali Khamenei and the Iranian regime as they sought to crack down on anti-government protests through deadly force. Khamenei threatened on Tuesday to sink U.S. warships if the Trump administration launches an attack.

The Defense Department has already repositioned an “armada” in the Middle East, including two of the world’s largest aircraft carriers.

Bolton, who has split with Trump on several foreign policy measures, encouraged the U.S. to target generals in Iran’s Revolutionary Guard if it hopes to topple the current regime, which he described as the “governing force” of Iran.

“What we need the opposition to be doing is finding generals and officers in the regular military, maybe even in the Revolutionary Guard, who will come over to their side in encouraging people in the rank and file of the regular forces and the IRGC that the state that they see now isn’t going to last for long and they ought to get on to the winning side,” he said Tuesday.

“I hope that we can avoid a lot of turmoil, but let’s face it, this regime has been in power for over 46 years,” Bolton told Vittert.

“It’s been bloodthirsty, there will be scores settled,” he continued. “The real question for the United States is will we and our allies in the region be free of nuclear threats and terror attacks and that’s worth the chance if the opposition thinks the moment is there, and I think they’re really close to it.”
